personality.role: co-founder and only backend engineer awake at this hour
personality.demeanor: matter-of-fact, knows the codebase intimately but missed this configuration
personality.recurring_concern: customer refund requests and financial impact of duplicate charges

facts: 8 more duplicate charge reports while investigating
Stripe shows two successful charges for each affected order
refund queue in support tool growing
inventory system shows negative stock for organic avocados and oat milk
three delivery drivers assigned the same order
two customers posted about it on the Austin subreddit
Lambda function code is correct for a single execution
the problem is the same message being processed more than once -- look at the queue configuration

hints.hint: how long does the Lambda function take to process a large order? check CloudWatch duration metrics
what happens to an SQS message if the consumer does not delete it within the visibility timeout?
compare the SQS visibility timeout with the Lambda function timeout -- is there a mismatch?
visibility timeout is 30 seconds, Lambda timeout is 90 seconds -- for large orders taking 60+ seconds, SQS makes the message visible again while Lambda is still processing it
set visibility timeout to at least 6x Lambda timeout (540 seconds per AWS recommendation), configure a dead-letter queue, add idempotency checks using order ID

glossary.SQS (Simple Queue Service): A managed message queue. One part of your system puts messages in; another part reads them out and acts on them. The queue holds messages until they are processed.
glossary.visibility timeout: The number of seconds a message stays hidden after a consumer picks it up. If the consumer does not delete the message before the timeout expires, SQS assumes processing failed and makes the message visible again for another consumer.
glossary.dead-letter queue (DLQ): A separate queue that catches messages which failed processing too many times. Instead of retrying forever, the message gets moved here so an engineer can inspect it.
glossary.Lambda function timeout: The maximum number of seconds AWS allows a Lambda function to run before forcefully stopping it. If the function finishes before this limit, it returns normally.
glossary.idempotency: A property of an operation meaning that running it more than once produces the same result as running it once. For example, charging a customer only once even if the charge request arrives twice.
glossary.at-least-once delivery: SQS standard queues guarantee every message is delivered at least once, but the same message may be delivered more than once. Your code must handle duplicates.
glossary.maxReceiveCount: A setting on the redrive policy that controls how many times a message can be received from the source queue before SQS moves it to the dead-letter queue.
glossary.event source mapping: The connection between an SQS queue and a Lambda function. AWS polls the queue on your behalf and invokes the Lambda function with new messages.
glossary.batch size: The number of messages Lambda pulls from the queue in a single invocation. A batch size of 1 means Lambda processes one message per invocation.

narrative_arc.call: alert arrives, stakes established from story.md Opening
narrative_arc.threshold: player enters investigation, Lambda concurrent executions spiking beyond order volume, invocation count higher than orders placed
narrative_arc.trials: queue visibility timeout is 30 seconds, Lambda timeout is 90 seconds, large orders take 60-90 seconds -- the numbers do not add up, duplicate charges only on large orders
narrative_arc.revelation: at the 30-second mark SQS makes the message visible again while Lambda is still processing it, second invocation picks it up, both complete, no idempotency check
narrative_arc.return: visibility timeout increased to 540 seconds, dead-letter queue configured, idempotency check added, refunds issued to affected customers

components.name: API Gateway
components.role: Receives orders from mobile and web clients and routes them to the validation Lambda
components.failure_impact: If the API Gateway fails, no new orders can be placed

components.name: canopy-api Lambda
components.role: Validates incoming orders and sends a message to the SQS queue for asynchronous processing
components.failure_impact: If validation fails, the order is rejected before entering the queue

components.name: canopy-order-queue (SQS standard queue)
components.role: Holds order messages with a 30-second visibility timeout, decoupling order submission from processing
components.failure_impact: If visibility timeout is shorter than processing time, messages become visible again and are processed a second time

components.name: canopy-process-order Lambda
components.role: Polls the queue (batch size 1) and processes each order: inventory check, Stripe payment, delivery slot reservation, confirmation email
components.failure_impact: If invoked twice for the same message, it produces duplicate charges, duplicate delivery slots, and negative inventory

components.name: DynamoDB
components.role: Stores order records for tracking and fulfillment
components.failure_impact: If DynamoDB writes fail, order state is lost and fulfillment cannot proceed

components.name: Stripe API
components.role: Handles payment charges for confirmed orders
components.failure_impact: If charged twice for the same order, customers see duplicate charges and request refunds

components.name: Internal delivery service
components.role: Reserves driver time slots for order delivery
components.failure_impact: If reserved twice, multiple drivers are assigned the same order

components.name: SES
components.role: Sends order confirmation emails to customers
components.failure_impact: If triggered twice, customers receive duplicate confirmation emails

system_narration.data_flow: Order enters through API Gateway, is validated by canopy-api, placed in SQS queue, picked up by canopy-process-order Lambda (batch size 1), which sequentially checks inventory in DynamoDB, charges the customer via Stripe, reserves a delivery slot, and sends a confirmation email via SES.
system_narration.what_broke: The SQS visibility timeout (30 seconds) was shorter than the Lambda processing time for large orders (60-90 seconds). When the timeout expired, SQS made the message visible again while the first invocation was still running. A second invocation picked up the same message. Both ran to completion, producing duplicate charges, duplicate delivery slots, and negative inventory counts. No dead-letter queue existed to catch repeatedly failing messages. No idempotency logic existed to detect that an order had already been processed.

resolution.root_cause: The SQS queue visibility timeout (30 seconds) was shorter than the Lambda function's execution time for large orders (60-90 seconds), causing SQS to redeliver messages while the original invocation was still processing them -- resulting in duplicate payment charges, duplicate delivery slot reservations, and negative inventory counts.

fix_criteria.description: Player identifies that the SQS visibility timeout (30s) is shorter than the Lambda processing time for large orders (60-90s)
Player connects the timeout mismatch to the duplicate charges and inventory issues
Player proposes increasing visibility timeout to at least 6x the Lambda timeout
Player proposes configuring a dead-letter queue for messages that fail processing

learning_objectives: SQS visibility timeout must be at least 6x the consumer's processing time (AWS recommendation) to prevent duplicate delivery
Dead-letter queues capture messages that fail processing after maxReceiveCount attempts, preventing infinite retry loops
Idempotency in message consumers: use a unique identifier (order ID) to detect and skip duplicate processing
sop_steps: Find which message queue feeds the Lambda function that processes orders. The connection between a queue and a Lambda function is called an event source mapping. Use the Lambda console or CLI to identify the queue name.

Compare two timeout settings: the queue's visibility timeout (how long a message stays hidden after a worker picks it up) and the Lambda function's timeout (the maximum time the function is allowed to run). AWS recommends the visibility timeout be at least 6 times the Lambda timeout. If it is shorter, messages can reappear while the function is still working on them.

Check CloudWatch metrics to confirm redelivery is happening. Compare NumberOfMessagesReceived (how many times messages were picked up) to NumberOfMessagesSent (how many messages were added). If the received count is higher than the sent count, messages are being picked up more than once.
Search CloudWatch Logs for the Lambda function. Look for the same order ID showing up in multiple invocations with different request IDs. This confirms the same order was processed more than once.

Increase the visibility timeout to at least 6 times the Lambda timeout. For a Lambda with a 90-second timeout, set the visibility timeout to at least 540 seconds. Use aws sqs set-queue-attributes to make the change.

Create a dead-letter queue (DLQ) -- a separate queue that catches messages which fail processing too many times. Configure a redrive policy on the main queue with maxReceiveCount set to 3, meaning a message gets three chances before being moved to the DLQ for human review.

Add a CloudWatch alarm on the DLQ's ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible metric. Any message landing in the DLQ means something went wrong, so set the threshold to 1 and have it notify the team immediately.

Add duplicate-detection logic (called idempotency) to the Lambda function. Before charging the customer or updating inventory, check DynamoDB for the order ID. If the order has already been processed, skip the side effects and return success. This protects against duplicates even if the queue delivers the same message twice.
Verify the fix by monitoring the received-to-sent ratio in CloudWatch over the next day. Under normal operation it should be approximately 1:1.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Lambda times out before finishing, causing failed orders instead of duplicate orders
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: In this sim, the Lambda had enough time to finish (90-second timeout). The problem was that the queue's visibility timeout (30 seconds) expired first, so a second copy of the function started on the same message. If the Lambda timeout were too short instead, the function would be killed mid-work. The message would return to the queue, eventually land in a dead-letter queue (if one existed), and the customer's order would fail rather than get charged twice.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Set the Lambda timeout to cover the slowest expected execution (look at the p99 duration metric). Monitor the Duration metric against the configured timeout to catch functions that are getting close to the limit.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Using a FIFO queue causes a throughput bottleneck
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: A FIFO (first-in, first-out) queue guarantees each message is processed exactly once and in order, which would have prevented the double-charge in this sim. But FIFO queues process messages in groups. If every order shares the same group ID, throughput is capped at 300 messages per second for that group. The symptom is orders piling up in the queue and processing delays -- not duplicates.
related_failure_modes.prevention: If you use a FIFO queue, assign different group IDs (for example, by customer or by region) so orders from different groups can be processed in parallel. Watch the ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage metric for growing backlogs.

related_failure_modes.scenario: A broken message retries forever because there is no dead-letter queue
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: In this sim, messages were processed successfully -- just processed twice. A different problem is a 'poison-pill' message with bad data that always causes the function to crash. Without a dead-letter queue, this message stays in the main queue forever, getting retried endlessly. It wastes Lambda capacity and blocks other messages from being processed. The symptom is errors and a growing backlog, not duplicate charges.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Always set up a dead-letter queue with a maxReceiveCount between 3 and 5. Add a CloudWatch alarm on the DLQ message count so you know immediately when something fails.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Lambda concurrency limit causes orders to wait in the queue
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: If the Lambda function is limited to, say, 5 simultaneous copies but the queue has 50 messages waiting, AWS throttles the function and SQS slows down its polling. Messages sit in the queue longer but each one is only processed once. The symptom is slow order processing and spikes in the Lambda Throttles metric, not duplicate charges.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Set the reserved concurrency (the maximum number of simultaneous function copies) proportional to your expected order volume. Monitor the Throttles metric and the age of the oldest message in the queue.

sop_practices: Always set the SQS visibility timeout to at least 6 times the Lambda function timeout when they are connected via an event source mapping. This ensures the message stays hidden long enough for the function to finish, even if it runs slowly or retries internally.

Set up a dead-letter queue on every production SQS queue, with maxReceiveCount between 3 and 5. Add a CloudWatch alarm that fires when any message lands in the DLQ, so your team is notified immediately about processing failures.

Design every message consumer to be idempotent -- meaning processing the same message twice produces the same result as processing it once. Before performing side effects like charging a customer or updating inventory, check whether the operation has already been completed using a unique identifier like the order ID.

Monitor the ratio of messages received to messages sent in CloudWatch. Under normal conditions, each message should be received approximately once. A ratio above 1 indicates messages are being redelivered, which points to a visibility timeout problem or repeated processing failures.
